title: Acids, Bases and Salts
summary: Chapter 2 explores the chemical properties of acids, bases, and salts. Students learn how acids produce H⁺ ions and bases produce OH⁻ ions in water. The pH scale is introduced to measure acidity and alkalinity. Important salts like baking soda, washing soda, bleaching powder, and plaster of Paris are studied with their chemical names, formulae, and uses. The concept of acid-base indicators and the preparation of common salts through neutralisation are also covered.
learningPoints: Define acids as substances that produce H⁺ (H₃O⁺) ions in water
Define bases as substances that produce OH⁻ ions in water
Distinguish strong acids (HCl, H₂SO₄, HNO₃) from weak acids (CH₃COOH)
Detect acids and bases using litmus, phenolphthalein, and universal indicator
Explain pH scale: pH < 7 = acidic, pH = 7 = neutral, pH > 7 = basic
State the neutralisation reaction: acid + base → salt + water
Explain reaction of acids with metals, metal oxides, metal carbonates
Explain reaction of bases with metals and non-metallic oxides
Identify common salts: NaCl (common salt), NaHCO₃ (baking soda), Na₂CO₃ (washing soda), Ca(OCl)Cl (bleaching powder), CaSO₄·½H₂O (plaster of Paris)
Explain the chlor-alkali process and its products
Define water of crystallisation with examples
Explain hardness of water and its removal

laws.name: pH Scale
laws.statement: pH measures hydrogen ion concentration. pH < 7 = acidic, pH = 7 = neutral, pH > 7 = basic/alkaline. Lower the pH, stronger the acid.

laws.name: Neutralisation
laws.statement: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. This reaction is always exothermic. The products depend on the acid and base used.

formulas.label: Neutralisation
formulas.description: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O. Always exothermic.

formulas.label: Acid + Metal
formulas.description: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂. Hydrogen gas is liberated.

formulas.label: Acid + Metal Carbonate
formulas.expression: Acid + Metal Carbonate → Salt + H₂O + CO₂↑

formulas.label: Chlor-Alkali Process
formulas.description: Electrolysis of brine produces NaOH, chlorine and hydrogen.

formulas.label: Baking Soda
formulas.description: Used in baking, antacids. Reacts with acid to release CO₂.

formulas.label: Washing Soda
formulas.description: Used in glass, soap, paper industry. Softens hard water.

formulas.label: Plaster of Paris
formulas.description: Sets hard when mixed with water. Used for plaster casts and sculptures.

formulas.label: Bleaching Powder
formulas.description: Made by passing Cl₂ over dry Ca(OH)₂. Used as disinfectant.

diagrams.title: pH Scale
diagrams.description: 0-14 scale showing colours of universal indicator. Red=strongly acidic, Green=neutral, Violet=strongly basic.

diagrams.title: Chlor-Alkali Electrolysis Cell
diagrams.description: Electrolysis of brine: Cl₂ at anode (right), H₂ at cathode (left), NaOH formed in solution.

resources.label: Karnataka SSLC Science Textbook
resources.description: Official Karnataka State Board Science textbook. Chapter 2 covers Acids, Bases and Salts.

resources.label: Acids, Bases and Salts — Video Lessons
resources.description: Khan Academy videos on pH, neutralisation, and common salts.

pyqs.question: Four solutions A, B, C and D showed pH as 5, 2, 7 and 11 respectively. Which solution (i) has more hydronium ions? (ii) is neutral? (iii) is the weak acid?
pyqs.answer: (i) B (pH 2 — most acidic, most H₃O⁺) (ii) C (pH 7 = neutral) (iii) A (pH 5 — weak acid)

pyqs.question: How do you detect a base using blue litmus paper?
pyqs.answer: The colour of blue litmus paper does not change when dipped in a basic solution. (Blue litmus stays blue in base; red litmus turns blue in base.)

pyqs.question: Name the salts responsible for the hardness of water.
pyqs.answer: Calcium salts (CaSO₄, CaCl₂, Ca(HCO₃)₂) and Magnesium salts (MgSO₄, MgCl₂, Mg(HCO₃)₂).

pyqs.question: What is acid rain?
pyqs.answer: When the pH of rain water is less than 5.6, it is called acid rain. It is caused by SO₂ and NO₂ dissolved in rainwater forming sulphuric and nitric acids.

pyqs.question: Hydrochloric acid is considered a strong acid. Why?
pyqs.answer: In water, hydrochloric acid completely dissociates into H⁺ and Cl⁻ ions. It forms more H⁺ ions in water — hence it is a strong acid.

pyqs.question: Write the chemical name and molecular formula of baking soda.
pyqs.answer: Chemical name: Sodium hydrogen carbonate (or Sodium bicarbonate). Molecular formula: NaHCO₃.

mcqs.question: Four solutions A(pH=5), B(pH=2), C(pH=7), D(pH=11). Which has more hydronium ions?
mcqs.explanation: Lower pH = more H₃O⁺ ions. pH 2 is most acidic = most hydronium ions.

mcqs.question: When pH of rainwater is less than 5.6, it is called:
mcqs.explanation: Normal rainwater has pH ~5.6 due to dissolved CO₂. When pH falls below 5.6 due to SO₂/NO₂, it is acid rain.

mcqs.question: The chemical name of bleaching powder is:
mcqs.explanation: Bleaching powder is Ca(OCl)Cl — calcium oxychloride. Made by Cl₂ + Ca(OH)₂.

mcqs.question: Which gas is liberated when an acid reacts with a metal carbonate?
mcqs.explanation: Na₂CO₃ + 2HCl → 2NaCl + H₂O + CO₂↑. CO₂ turns lime water milky.

mcqs.question: Plaster of Paris sets hard because:
options: B. It gains water of crystallisation
mcqs.explanation: CaSO₄·½H₂O + 1½H₂O → CaSO₄·2H₂O. It absorbs water to form hard gypsum.

mcqs.question: The product formed when NaOH reacts with CO₂ is:
mcqs.explanation: 2NaOH + CO₂ → Na₂CO₃ + H₂O. Base reacts with non-metallic oxide to form salt and water.

mcqs.question: In the chlor-alkali process, products formed are:
pH of a neutral solution is:
mcqs.explanation: pH = 7 is neutral. Pure water at 25°C has pH = 7.

mcqs.question: Washing soda is:
mcqs.explanation: Washing soda is Na₂CO₃·10H₂O (sodium carbonate decahydrate). Used to soften hard water.

mcqs.question: Salts responsible for hardness of water are:
mcqs.explanation: Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions from their bicarbonates, sulphates, and chlorides cause hardness of water.

mcqs.question: The simplest hydrocarbon is:
mcqs.explanation: Methane CH₄ is the simplest hydrocarbon — one carbon with four hydrogen atoms.

mcqs.question: Antacids work because they:
mcqs.explanation: Antacids contain Mg(OH)₂ or Al(OH)₃ which neutralise excess HCl in stomach (neutralisation reaction).

mcqs.question: Which indicator turns red in acid and blue in base?
mcqs.explanation: Litmus is red in acid and blue in base. Phenolphthalein is colourless in acid and pink in base.

mcqs.question: Water of crystallisation in washing soda is:
mcqs.explanation: Na₂CO₃·10H₂O — washing soda has 10 molecules of water of crystallisation.

mcqs.question: Tooth decay is caused by:
mcqs.explanation: When pH in mouth falls below 5.5, tooth enamel erodes. Bacteria produce acids from sugar — causing decay.

exercises.question: What happens when dilute HCl is added to zinc? Write the equation and name the type of reaction.
exercises.answer: Zn + 2HCl → ZnCl₂ + H₂↑. Hydrogen gas is evolved (tested by burning — squeaky pop). This is a displacement reaction.

exercises.question: Explain the chlor-alkali process. What are the products and their uses?
exercises.answer: 2NaCl(aq) + 2H₂O → 2NaOH + Cl₂↑ + H₂↑. Products: (i) NaOH — soap, detergents, paper (ii) Cl₂ — PVC, disinfectants, bleaching powder (iii) H₂ — fuel, margarine.

exercises.question: Give three differences between strong acid and weak acid.
exercises.answer: Strong acid: completely dissociates (HCl, H₂SO₄, HNO₃), high H⁺ concentration, low pH. Weak acid: partially dissociates (CH₃COOH), low H⁺ concentration, higher pH.

exercises.question: What is plaster of Paris? How is it prepared? Write its uses.
exercises.answer: CaSO₄·½H₂O (calcium sulphate hemihydrate). Prepared by heating gypsum at 100°C: CaSO₄·2H₂O → CaSO₄·½H₂O + 1½H₂O. Uses: Plaster casts for fractures, making statues, sealing joints.

exercises.question: Why should bleaching powder not be mixed with acids?
exercises.answer: Ca(OCl)Cl + H₂SO₄ → CaSO₄ + Cl₂ + H₂O. Mixing with acid releases toxic chlorine gas which is harmful.

exercises.question: Why does a bee sting cause pain and how can it be treated?
exercises.answer: Bee sting injects formic acid (methanoic acid — acidic) causing pain. It can be treated by rubbing baking soda (NaHCO₃) solution which neutralises the acid.

exercises.question: Explain with diagram how pH affects plant growth and soil.
exercises.answer: Most crops grow best at pH 6-7. Acidic soil (pH < 6) is treated with lime (CaO or Ca(OH)₂) to raise pH. Alkaline soil is treated with gypsum (CaSO₄). Farmers test soil pH before choosing crops.

exercises.question: What is water of crystallisation? Give two examples.
exercises.answer: The fixed number of water molecules present in a crystalline salt is called water of crystallisation. Examples: (i) CuSO₄·5H₂O — blue vitriol (ii) Na₂CO₃·10H₂O — washing soda (iii) CaSO₄·2H₂O — gypsum.
